map.subtitle: Big Idea 3: Algorithms and Programming   •   2 days   •   LO AAP-3.F
map.intro: Everything for this topic lives in this folder. Topic 3.16 is the conceptual capstone of the programming strand — students build and then judge simulations — and it ships an interactive, auto-graded coding-practice page that reuses the very weather model they trace on paper. Here is how the surfaces fit together.
folderItems: Slides — Teacher Edition, Day 1 (what a simulation is; abstraction and removing detail; RANDOM and trials) and Day 2 (when to simulate; refining hypotheses; bias from included/excluded elements). Speaker notes on every slide.
Teacher Guide (two-day pacing with the QOTD warm-up, misconceptions, differentiation, CED alignment, auto-derived quiz key)
Guided Notes — Day 1 and Day 2 (student fill-in + answer KEY)
Exercise 1: Trace the Rain Simulation and Exercise 2: Audit for Bias (student + answer KEY)
Discussion — What Did the Model Leave Out? (3-part: Opening, Mid-lesson, Closing across the two days)
Quiz (student + answer KEY — 8 questions on LO AAP-3.F, canonical in the Day-1 master JSON)
Coding Practice answer KEY (reference solutions for the interactive weather-simulation problems; students only ever see stub starters online)
map.surfacesHeading: How the three surfaces fit together
surfaces: Slides = the instruction you project and teach from (Day 1: building a simulation as an abstraction driven by RANDOM; Day 2: judging it — when to simulate and where bias hides). All code is shown in exam pseudocode next to runnable Python.
This folder = your private scaffolding — keys, pacing, and facilitation that are deliberately NOT posted to the site, including the coding-practice reference solutions.

Website = student practice that paper cannot do: the coding page runs the same weather model against a DETERMINISTIC check (a property or summary of many trials, never a raw random draw), so RANDOM's variability never breaks the auto-grader. The exercises and quiz grade instantly with per-answer feedback. Student versions live online; the answer keys stay with you.

Scheduling: Topic 3.16 is a 2-day topic. The quiz runs as the opener of the NEXT class (spaced retrieval), so the topic never silently consumes a third day. Topic 3.17 (Algorithmic Efficiency) begins immediately after the quiz.

exercise1.title: Trace the Rain Simulation
exercise1.intro: You are a quality tester for the weather app built in class. The simulation reduces all of real weather to a single 'chance of rain' number and lets RANDOM decide each day. Your job is to TRACE it by hand — determine each day's outcome and the running rainy-day count from the given rolls — then reason about what is PREDICTABLE and what is not. This is the core LO AAP-3.F part-a skill: a single trial varies, but properties across many trials (like the rainy fraction) are stable and let you draw inferences.
exercise1.scenarioTitle: The Model: One Number, One Random Roll per Day
exercise1.narrative: The model is: chanceOfRain ← 40. For each day, roll ← RANDOM(1, 100); the day is 'rain' if roll ≤ 40, otherwise 'shine'; a counter rainy tracks how many rainy days have occurred. Below is a log of 8 pre-recorded rolls (so your trace is checkable). Work your own trace on scratch paper before answering Part A — remember the rule uses ≤, so a roll of exactly 40 counts as rain.
exercise1.scenarioNote: Model built in the Day 1 lesson (chanceOfRain = 40); notation follows the AP Computer Science Principles Exam Reference Sheet (Effective Fall 2023). RANDOM(1, 100) returns an equally likely integer from 1 to 100.
exercise1.predict: Before you answer Part A, predict in one sentence: across these 8 days, will the rainy FRACTION equal exactly 0.40? Why or why not?

partA.q: Using the rolls in the log, confirm the outcome (rain/shine) for days 3 and 6, and give the running rainy-day count after each. Explain what decides each day's outcome.
partA.answer: Day 3: roll = 40. The rule is rain if roll ≤ 40, and 40 ≤ 40 is true, so day 3 is RAIN; the rainy count goes from 1 to 2. Day 6: roll = 41. Since 41 > 40, the condition roll ≤ 40 is false, so day 6 is SHINE; the count stays at 3. What decides each outcome is the single random roll compared against the one kept parameter, chanceOfRain (40) — RANDOM supplies the day-to-day variability that stands in for everything the model deleted (EK AAP-3.F.8), and each new roll is a new value in the simulation's changing state (EK AAP-3.F.2). Full credit requires naming the ≤ boundary as the reason 40 is rain but 41 is shine.

partA.q: After all 8 days, how many were rainy, and what rainy FRACTION does that give? The model's built-in chance is 40% (0.40). Explain why the traced fraction does not equal 0.40, and what would make it closer.
partA.answer: 4 of the 8 days were rainy, a fraction of 4/8 = 0.50. The built-in chance is 0.40, but the traced fraction is 0.50 because 8 days is a tiny, noisy sample — with so few trials, the random rolls happen to land ≤ 40 half the time here (EK AAP-3.F.2: each run moves through its own varying set of values). Running many MORE days would make the fraction settle close to 0.40, because each RANDOM value is equally likely and the noise averages out over many trials. That gap — a noisy single run versus a stable long-run inference — is exactly why real simulations run thousands of trials before drawing a conclusion (EK AAP-3.F.3).

partA.q: This model represents 'weather' with just one number and a random roll. Name two real details it removed, and explain why removing them is the correct way to DEVELOP a simulation rather than a flaw.
partA.answer: The model removed essentially all of real weather except a chance of rain — for example, temperature, humidity, wind, air pressure, cloud cover, or fronts (any two). Removing them is correct because developing an abstract simulation IS the process of removing specific details and simplifying functionality (EK AAP-3.F.4). The purpose — estimating how often it rains — needs only the rain frequency, so the deleted details would add complexity without serving the goal. A model that kept every detail would be as impossible to run as the real sky (EK AAP-3.F.1), so it could never be run the thousands of times an inference requires. The simplification is what makes the model useful, not a shortcut that weakens it.

partA.q: A tester runs the real program (not this fixed log) twice and gets rainy fractions of 0.38 and 0.43. She reports a bug. Using the idea of 'changing state,' explain why two different results are correct behavior, not a bug.
partA.answer: Two different results are exactly what a correct simulation produces. Each run draws a fresh sequence of RANDOM values, so the simulation moves through a different changing state every time (EK AAP-3.F.2), and the rainy fraction will land somewhere near — but rarely exactly on — the built-in 0.40. Getting 0.38 one run and 0.43 the next is normal variability, not a defect; RANDOM is deliberately modeling the day-to-day unpredictability of real weather. A bug would be a fraction that is impossible (below 0 or above 1) or one that never varies at all. The trustworthy result is the inference that rain occurs about 40% of the time, which both runs support.

exercise1.partBIntro: Answer in complete sentences using the CED vocabulary. These are the same items available on the site exercise.

partB.q: Suppose you change chanceOfRain from 40 to 70 and rerun the 1000-day simulation. Predict how the rainy fraction changes, and explain how this single change lets you test a hypothesis without any real weather.
partB.answer: With chanceOfRain = 70, each day rains when the roll is ≤ 70 instead of ≤ 40, so far more days are rainy; the rainy fraction over 1000 days will settle near 0.70. Changing that one value and re-running is a controlled experiment: you have tested the hypothesis 'a higher rain chance produces more rainy days' and can compare the two fractions directly, all in seconds and without waiting for or affecting any real weather. This is how simulations facilitate the formulation and refinement of hypotheses (EK AAP-3.F.7) and let you investigate a phenomenon without the constraints of the real world (EK AAP-3.F.3).

partB.q: The chanceOfRain of 40 came from ONE weather station downtown, using ONLY last summer's data. Name one real-world element this excludes, and explain the bias it could introduce when the app is used across the whole city in winter.
partB.answer: Using one downtown station and only last summer's data excludes, for example, the season (winter weather differs from summer), the city's other microclimates (a coastal or hilltop neighborhood may rain far more or less than downtown), and any long-term climate trend (any one of these is acceptable). The bias: the model's 40% chance reflects summer, downtown conditions, so used across the whole city in winter it will systematically mis-predict — likely too low for a rainy-winter neighborhood, too high for a dry one. This is bias derived from the real-world elements the designers included or excluded (EK AAP-3.F.5); the model runs perfectly and prints confident numbers, but it is blind to everything it never sampled.

partB.q: ENRICHMENT: Give one real phenomenon where a rain-style simulation would be the RIGHT tool because the real experiment is impractical, and name which of the CED's reasons (too big, small, fast, slow, expensive, or dangerous) applies.
partB.answer: Many answers work. Example: simulating how a wildfire spreads through a forest under different wind conditions — the real experiment is TOO DANGEROUS (and too expensive) to run for real, so a simulation lets fire crews investigate strategies safely (EK AAP-3.F.6). Other valid examples: the long-term climate of a region (too slow), the motion of a galaxy (too big), or a disease outbreak across a country (too dangerous/too big). Full credit requires a phenomenon plus a clearly matched impracticality reason, showing the student can judge WHEN a simulation is appropriate (EK AAP-3.F.1).

partB.q: Next step: you traced this simulation by hand. Now head to the Topic 3.16 page and type and run the harder versions, where the computer checks a deterministic property of your simulation for you.
partB.answer: (Hand-off to the online coding practice — no written answer needed.)

exercise2.title: Audit for Bias
exercise2.subtitle: 5 simulations - find what was left out and how it biases the result
exercise2.intro: You are on a review board that signs off on simulations before anyone acts on their results. For each simulation below, your job is to COMPARE it to the real world it claims to represent: decide whether a simulation is even the right tool, name a real-world element the designers left out, and state how that exclusion biases the results. This is LO AAP-3.F part b. Vague answers like 'it's not accurate' score zero — name the excluded element and the direction of the bias.
exercise2.standard: CB Standard (required): is a simulation appropriate here + one specific EXCLUDED real-world element + how that exclusion biases the result, for all 5 cases. Enrichment (optional): note how the SAME simplification is both reasonable (F.4) and a bias risk (F.5).
exercise2.methodHeading: Three-Step Method - use for every simulation
method: Step 1 - Is a simulation the right tool?
Ask whether the real experiment is impractical — too big, small, fast, slow, expensive, or dangerous (EK AAP-3.F.6). If reality is cheap, fast, and safe to test, a simulation may be the wrong choice.
List the real-world elements the model does not represent. The bias almost always hides here, not in the polished output (EK AAP-3.F.5). Ask: whose data, which conditions, which cases are missing?
Step 3 - State the direction of the bias
Say how the exclusion skews the result — too high, too low, blind to a group, wrong under certain conditions. 'Biased' is not an answer; 'biased toward X because Y was left out' is.
exercise2.enrichmentNote: Enrichment (the F.4/F.5 tension): every simplification that makes a model runnable (EK AAP-3.F.4) is simultaneously an exclusion that can bias it (EK AAP-3.F.5). The skill is not to avoid simplifying — that is impossible — but to know which cut details are harmless for the purpose and which quietly distort the answer. When you finish, move to the Topic 3.16 coding page to build and run the weather model, grading a deterministic property of many trials.
fields: Is a simulation the right tool here? (impractical because...):
One real-world element the model EXCLUDES:
Enrichment — how that same cut is both reasonable and a bias risk:

scenarios.text: A car company simulates a new bumper in 10,000 virtual crashes at different speeds before building a single physical prototype, to decide which shapes to test for real.

key.signs: A simulation is clearly the right tool: crash-testing 10,000 real cars would be far TOO EXPENSIVE (and slow), which is exactly the impracticality EK AAP-3.F.6 describes. The simulation lets engineers investigate many designs without the real-world cost.
key.mechanism: A likely EXCLUDED element is real-world material imperfections — a physical bumper has tiny flaws, weld variations, and temperature effects the model may idealize away (EK AAP-3.F.5). Other valid answers: unusual crash angles, road debris, or aged/worn materials.
key.subtype: The idealized-material cut is reasonable (EK AAP-3.F.4) for screening which shapes are promising, but it biases results OPTIMISTICALLY — the virtual bumper performs a bit better than a flawed real one will, which is exactly why the company still tests the finalists for real.

scenarios.text: A city simulates evacuation traffic using data collected only on ordinary weekday mornings, then uses it to plan for a hurricane evacuation.

key.signs: A simulation is appropriate — you cannot stage a real hurricane evacuation to experiment, it would be TOO DANGEROUS and expensive (EK AAP-3.F.6). The problem is not whether to simulate, but what data it was built from.
key.mechanism: The model EXCLUDES hurricane-specific conditions: everyone leaving at once, contraflow, panic, stalled or abandoned cars, and out-of-town traffic — none of which appear in ordinary weekday-morning data (EK AAP-3.F.5).
key.subtype: Using weekday data is a convenient simplification (EK AAP-3.F.4), but here it badly biases the result: the model will predict far smoother, faster evacuation than reality, underestimating the jams and putting lives at risk. The cut that is 'reasonable' for routine planning is dangerous for the actual purpose.

scenarios.text: A team decides whether a pocket coin is fair by writing a program that flips a simulated RANDOM coin a million times and reports the fraction of heads.

key.signs: This is the trap case: a simulation is arguably the WRONG tool for the stated goal. Testing whether a REAL coin is fair requires flipping the real coin — it is cheap, fast, and safe, so it is not impractical (EK AAP-3.F.6). A simulated coin only tests the model, not the physical coin.
key.mechanism: The simulation EXCLUDES the actual physical coin entirely — its weight distribution, worn edges, and how it is flipped (EK AAP-3.F.5). RANDOM was told to be fair, so the program will report roughly 50% heads no matter what the real coin does.
key.subtype: This shows the deepest version of the bias idea: assuming the coin is fair is baked into the model, so the 'result' just echoes that assumption. RANDOM producing a clean 50% feels objective (EK AAP-3.F.5) but proves nothing about the real coin — a perfect example of a confident output from a model that excluded the very thing being studied.

scenarios.text: A health agency simulates how a new disease spreads to decide on school closures, using a model based only on how the disease spread in a single dense downtown neighborhood.

key.signs: A simulation is the right tool: you cannot ethically let a disease spread to experiment — it is TOO DANGEROUS, and the timeline is TOO SLOW to wait out (EK AAP-3.F.6). Simulating lets officials draw inferences and refine hypotheses before acting (EK AAP-3.F.3, F.7).
key.mechanism: The model EXCLUDES places unlike that dense downtown neighborhood — rural areas, suburbs, different age mixes, and different contact patterns (EK AAP-3.F.5). It was calibrated on one setting and applied everywhere.
key.subtype: Fitting to one neighborhood is a necessary simplification (EK AAP-3.F.4), but it biases predictions: the model will overestimate spread in sparse rural areas and may misjudge age-related risk, so a one-size closure policy could be wrong for many communities. Same cut, reasonable in one place, biased when generalized.

scenarios.text: A streaming app simulates which new shows to greenlight by modeling viewer behavior using only the watch histories of its current subscribers.

key.signs: A simulation can help — testing greenlighting decisions on real productions is TOO EXPENSIVE to do repeatedly (EK AAP-3.F.6), so modeling viewer response first is reasonable, and reruns let the app refine hypotheses about what will succeed (EK AAP-3.F.7).
key.mechanism: The model EXCLUDES everyone who is NOT already a subscriber — potential new audiences with different tastes — and it excludes shows unlike anything current subscribers have watched (EK AAP-3.F.5). It only knows the people it already has.
key.subtype: Using existing subscribers is a natural simplification (EK AAP-3.F.4), but it biases the app toward MORE OF THE SAME: it will keep greenlighting shows like past hits and systematically undervalue anything that would attract a new audience it never modeled. The exclusion quietly shapes the company's entire catalog.

discussion.title: What Did the Model Leave Out?
discussion.minutes: ~20 min total - 3 parts - Opening (Day 1) + Mid-lesson (Day 1) + Closing (Day 2)
discussion.overview: A simulation is powerful precisely because it throws almost everything away — and dangerous for the same reason. Across three short windows in this topic, your group will argue about how much detail a good model should keep, when a simulation is the right tool at all, and how the choices a designer makes about what to include or exclude can bias results that look perfectly objective.

parts.name: Part 1: Opening Discussion
parts.when: Day 1, ~6 min, after the bell ringer
parts.intro: In your group of 3-4, discuss both questions (about 3 minutes each), then share your most interesting answer with the class. No written answers required - just discuss and listen.

items.text: Question 1. To answer the rain question, your group threw away temperature, wind, and clouds and kept one number. What made that OK — and can you name a question about weather where throwing those away would NOT be OK?

Question 2. Your pretend-weather can run 1000 days in a second, but any single day it predicts is basically a coin flip. If you can't trust one day, what exactly CAN you trust from the model, and why is that still useful to a city planner?
Our group's most interesting finding from Part 1 (write 1-2 sentences to share):

parts.name: Part 2: Mid-lesson Discussion
parts.when: Day 1, ~6 min, after the traced week
parts.intro: In your group, complete both tasks below.

items.label: Task A - Noisy day, steady answer
items.text: Your traced week rained about 57% of the time, but the model uses a 40% chance. In one sentence your whole group agrees on, explain what would happen to that percentage over 10,000 days, and why the model's answer gets MORE trustworthy as you run it more, not less.

items.label: Task B - How real is too real?
items.text: Debate for about 3 minutes: is it possible for a simulation to be TOO detailed and realistic? Name one downside of a model that tries to include almost everything, and decide as a group whether 'more realistic' always means 'more useful.'

parts.name: Part 3: Closing Debrief
parts.when: Day 2, ~8 min, after Common AP Traps
parts.intro: As a group, work the prompt below, then one member shares your verdict with the class.

items.label: The confident, biased model
items.text: A flood-risk model built only on the last 20 years of data tells a town it is safe to build on the floodplain, and it prints a crisp, confident number. As a group, write the clearest one-paragraph explanation of HOW a simulation can be biased and objective-looking at the same time — one a worried resident would understand — using the words 'excluded' and 'RANDOM.' Then name one real element you'd demand the model include before trusting it.
Our group's verdict (the single sentence we think best kills the 'a computer made it, so it's unbiased' misconception):
